Source: U.S. Decennial Census[[142]](#cite_note-DecennialCensus-146) Historical Population Figures[[143]](#cite_note-New_Orleans_2010-147)[[144]](#cite_note-2014CensusEst-148)[[145]](#cite_note-149)[[146]](#cite_note-PopEstCities-150)[[147]](#cite_note-DinHarkins1996-151)  1790–1960[[148]](#cite_note-152) 1900–1990[[149]](#cite_note-153) 1990–2000[[150]](#cite_note-154) 2010–2013[[151]](#cite_note-QF-155) 2020 estimate[[152]](#cite_note-USCensusEst2020-156) | | |

From the [2010 U.S. census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") to 2014 census estimates the city grew by 12%, adding an average of more than 10,000 new residents each year following the official decennial census.[[144]](#cite_note-2014CensusEst-148) According to the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), there were 383,997 people, 151,753 households, and 69,370 families residing in the city. Prior to 1960, the population of New Orleans steadily increased to a historic 627,525.

Beginning in 1960, the population decreased due to factors such as the cycles of oil production and tourism,[[153]](#cite_note-Hollander_2008-157)[[154]](#cite_note-Frey_1987-158) and as [suburbanization](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization"), diversity, and [white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight") increased (as with many U.S. cities),[[155]](#cite_note-Fussell_2007-159) and jobs migrated to surrounding parishes.[[156]](#cite_note-Katz_2006-160)[[157]](#cite_note-:6-161) This economic and population decline resulted in high levels of poverty in the city; in 1960 it had the fifth-highest poverty rate of all U.S. cities,[[158]](#cite_note-Spain_1979-162) and was almost twice the national average in 2005, at 24.5%.[[155]](#cite_note-Fussell_2007-159) New Orleans experienced an increase in [residential segregation](/wiki/Residential_segregation "Residential segregation") from 1900 to 1980, leaving the disproportionately Black and African American poor in older, low-lying locations.[[156]](#cite_note-Katz_2006-160)[[157]](#cite_note-:6-161) These areas were especially susceptible to flood and storm damage.[[159]](#cite_note-Kates_2006-163)

The last population estimate before Hurricane Katrina was 454,865, as of July 1, 2005.[[160]](#cite_note-164) A population analysis released in August 2007 estimated the population to be 273,000, 60% of the pre-Katrina population and an increase of about 50,000 since July 2006.[[161]](#cite_note-165) A September 2007 report by The Greater New Orleans Community Data Center, which tracks population based on U.S. Postal Service figures, found that in August 2007, just over 137,000 households received mail. That compares with about 198,000 households in July 2005, representing about 70% of pre-Katrina population.[[162]](#cite_note-166) In 2010, the U.S. Census Bureau revised upward its 2008 population estimate for the city, to 336,644 inhabitants.[[143]](#cite_note-New_Orleans_2010-147) Estimates from 2010 showed that neighborhoods that did not flood were near or even greater than 100% of their pre-Katrina populations.[[163]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-167)

Katrina displaced 800,000 people, contributing significantly to the decline.[[164]](#cite_note-Nelson_2011-168) Black and African Americans, renters, the elderly, and people with low income were disproportionately affected by Katrina, compared to affluent and White residents.[[165]](#cite_note-Nelson_2007-169)[[166]](#cite_note-Morse_2008-170) Those same groups also had the slowest growth rate in the city after Katrina primarily due to the rising cost of living, low performing public schools, and high crime in lower income neighborhoods.[[167]](#cite_note-171)[[168]](#cite_note-172)[[169]](#cite_note-173) In Katrina's aftermath, city government commissioned groups such as Bring New Orleans Back Commission, the New Orleans Neighborhood Rebuilding Plan, the Unified New Orleans Plan, and the Office of Recovery Management to contribute to plans addressing depopulation. Their ideas included shrinking the city's [footprint](/wiki/Ecological_footprint "Ecological footprint") from before the storm, incorporating community voices into development plans, and creating [green spaces](/wiki/Urban_open_space "Urban open space"),[[165]](#cite_note-Nelson_2007-169) some of which incited controversy.[[170]](#cite_note-Olshanski_2008-174)[[171]](#cite_note-Reardon_2008-175)

A 2006 study by researchers at [Tulane University](/wiki/Tulane_University "Tulane University") and the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), determined that as many as 10,000 to 14,000 [undocumented immigrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigration "Illegal immigration"), many from [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), resided in New Orleans.[[172]](#cite_note-undoc-176) In 2016, the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") estimated at least 35,000 undocumented immigrants lived in New Orleans and its metropolitan area.[[173]](#cite_note-177) The [New Orleans Police Department](/wiki/New_Orleans_Police_Department "New Orleans Police Department") began a new policy to "no longer cooperate with federal immigration enforcement" beginning on February 28, 2016.[[174]](#cite_note-178)

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_Orleans&action=edit), 90.3% of residents age five and older spoke English at home as a [primary language](/wiki/Primary_language "Primary language"), while 4.8% spoke Spanish, 1.9% Vietnamese, and 1.1% spoke French. In total, 9.7% population age five and older spoke a [mother language](/wiki/Mother_language "Mother language") other than English.[[175]](#cite_note-179)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Ethnic_Origins_in_New_Orleans.png/250px-Ethnic_Origins_in_New_Orleans.png)](/wiki/File:Ethnic_Origins_in_New_Orleans.png)

Ethnic origins in New Orleans (2020)

Growing into a predominantly Black and African-American city by race and ethnicity since 1990,[[178]](#cite_note-census1-182) in 2010 the racial and ethnic makeup of New Orleans was 60.2% Black and African American, 33.0% [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans"), 2.9% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans") (1.7% Vietnamese, 0.3% Indian, 0.3% Chinese, 0.1% Filipino, 0.1% Korean), 0.0% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_American "Pacific Islander American"), and 1.7% people of [two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans").[[188]](#cite_note-:3-193) People of [Hispanic or Latino American](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") origin made up 5.3% of the population; 1.3% were Mexican, 1.3% Honduran, 0.4% Cuban, 0.3% Puerto Rican, and 0.3% Nicaraguan.

In 2020, the racial and ethnic makeup of the city was 53.61% Black or African American, 31.61% [non-Hispanic white](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_white "Non-Hispanic white"), 0.2% [American Indian and Alaska Native](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 0.03% Pacific Islander, 3.71% multiracial or of another race, and 8.08% Hispanic and Latino American of any race.[[176]](#cite_note-:5-180) The growth of the Hispanic and Latino population in New Orleans proper from 2010 to 2020 reflected national demographic trends of diversification throughout regions once predominantly non-Hispanic white.[[189]](#cite_note-194) Additionally, the 2020 census revealed the city now has a more diverse population than it did before Katrina, yet 21% fewer people than it had in 2000.[[190]](#cite_note-195) Out of [Louisiana's 64 parishes](/wiki/List_of_parishes_in_Louisiana "List of parishes in Louisiana"), it is [one of six that have an African-American majority](/wiki/List_of_U.S._communities_with_African-American_majority_populations_in_2020#Counties "List of U.S. communities with African-American majority populations in 2020") as of the 2020 census.

As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_Orleans&action=edit), the Hispanic and Latino American population had also grown in the Greater New Orleans area alongside Black and African American residents, including in [Kenner](/wiki/Kenner,_Louisiana "Kenner, Louisiana"), central [Metairie](/wiki/Metairie,_Louisiana "Metairie, Louisiana"), and [Terrytown](/wiki/Terrytown,_Louisiana "Terrytown, Louisiana") in Jefferson Parish and Eastern New Orleans and Mid-City in New Orleans proper.[[191]](#cite_note-HispboomKENNER-196) [Janet Murguía](/wiki/Janet_Murgu%C3%ADa "Janet Murguía"), president and chief executive officer of the [UnidosUS](/wiki/UnidosUS "UnidosUS"), stated that up to 120,000 Hispanic and Latino Americans workers lived in New Orleans. In June 2007, one study stated that the Hispanic and Latino American population had risen from 15,000, pre-Katrina, to over 50,000.[[192]](#cite_note-197)

After Katrina the small [Brazilian American](/wiki/Brazilian_Americans "Brazilian Americans") population expanded. Portuguese speakers were the second most numerous group to take [English as a second language](/wiki/English_as_a_second_language "English as a second language") classes in the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New Orleans, after Spanish speakers. Many Brazilians worked in skilled trades such as tile and flooring, although fewer worked as day laborers than other Hispanic and Latino Americans. Many had moved from Brazilian communities in the [northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"), and Florida and Georgia. Brazilians settled throughout the metropolitan area; most were undocumented. In January 2008, the New Orleans Brazilian population had a mid-range estimate of 3,000 people. By 2008, Brazilians had opened many small churches, shops and restaurants catering to their community.[[193]](#cite_note-198)

Among the growing [Asian American](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans") community, the earliest [Filipino Americans](/wiki/Filipino_Americans "Filipino Americans") to live within the city arrived in the early 1800s.[[194]](#cite_note-199) The Vietnamese American community grew to become the largest by 2010 as many fled the aftermath of the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") in the 1970s.[[195]](#cite_note-200)

### Sexual orientation and gender identity

See also: [LGBTQ rights in Louisiana](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_Louisiana "LGBTQ rights in Louisiana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Bourbon_Pub_%26_Parade_-_We_stand_in_pride_with_Pulse_Nightclub_-_New_Orleans_Pride_Parade_2016_%2827762662225%29.jpg/250px-Bourbon_Pub_%26_Parade_-_We_stand_in_pride_with_Pulse_Nightclub_-_New_Orleans_Pride_Parade_2016_%2827762662225%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bourbon_Pub_%26_Parade_-_We_stand_in_pride_with_Pulse_Nightclub_-_New_Orleans_Pride_Parade_2016_(27762662225).jpg)

2016 New Orleans Pride

New Orleans and its metropolitan area have historically been popular destinations for [lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") communities.[[196]](#cite_note-201)[[197]](#cite_note-202) In 2015, a [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_(company) "Gallup (company)") survey determined New Orleans was one of the largest cities in the American South with a significant LGBT population.[[198]](#cite_note-203)[[199]](#cite_note-204) Much of the LGBT community in New Orleans lives near the Central Business District, Mid-City, and Uptown; several gay bars and nightclubs are present in those areas.[[200]](#cite_note-205)

### Religion

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Stlouiscathedralnight.jpg/250px-Stlouiscathedralnight.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stlouiscathedralnight.jpg)

[Cathedral-Basilica of St. Louis, King of France](/wiki/St._Louis_Cathedral,_New_Orleans "St. Louis Cathedral, New Orleans")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/BethIsraelCarondeletFrontA.JPG/250px-BethIsraelCarondeletFrontA.JPG)](/wiki/File:BethIsraelCarondeletFrontA.JPG)

Beth Israel synagogue building on Carondelet Street

New Orleans's colonial history of French and Spanish settlement generated a strong [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") tradition. Catholic missions ministered to slaves and free people of color and established schools for them. In addition, many late 19th and early 20th century European immigrants, such as the Irish, some Germans, and Italians were Catholic. Within the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New Orleans](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_New_Orleans "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New Orleans") (which includes not only the city but the surrounding parishes as well), 40% percent of the population was Roman Catholic since 2016.[[201]](#cite_note-206) Catholicism is reflected in French and Spanish cultural traditions, including its many [parochial schools](/wiki/Parochial_schools "Parochial schools"), street names, architecture and festivals, including [Mardi Gras](/wiki/New_Orleans_Mardi_Gras "New Orleans Mardi Gras"). Within the city and metropolitan area, [Catholicism](/wiki/Black_Catholicism "Black Catholicism") is also reflected in the Black and African cultural traditions with Gospel mass.[[202]](#cite_note-207) The statue of [Our Lady of Prompt Succor](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Prompt_Succor "Our Lady of Prompt Succor") is a notable symbol of the Catholic faith in New Orleans and throughout Louisiana.

Influenced by the [Bible Belt](/wiki/Bible_Belt "Bible Belt")'s prominent [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") population, New Orleans also has a sizable non-Catholic Christian demographic. Roughly the majority of Protestant Christians were [Baptist](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"), and the city proper's largest non-Catholic bodies were the [Southern Baptist Convention](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention"), the [National Missionary Baptist Convention of America](/wiki/National_Missionary_Baptist_Convention_of_America "National Missionary Baptist Convention of America"), [non-denominationals](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity"), the [National Baptist Convention](/wiki/National_Baptist_Convention,_USA,_Inc. "National Baptist Convention, USA, Inc."), the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church"), the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"), the [African Methodist Episcopal Church](/wiki/African_Methodist_Episcopal_Church "African Methodist Episcopal Church"), the [National Baptist Convention of America](/wiki/National_Baptist_Convention_of_America_International,_Inc. "National Baptist Convention of America International, Inc."), and the [Church of God in Christ](/wiki/Church_of_God_in_Christ "Church of God in Christ") according to the [Association of Religion Data Archives](/wiki/Association_of_Religion_Data_Archives "Association of Religion Data Archives") in 2020.[[203]](#cite_note-:22-208)

New Orleans displays a distinctive variety of [Louisiana Voodoo](/wiki/Louisiana_Voodoo "Louisiana Voodoo"), due in part to [syncretism](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") with African and Afro-Caribbean Roman Catholic beliefs. The fame of voodoo practitioner [Marie Laveau](/wiki/Marie_Laveau "Marie Laveau") contributed to this, as did New Orleans's Caribbean cultural influences.[[204]](#cite_note-209)[[205]](#cite_note-210)[[206]](#cite_note-BostonGlobeNorthernmost-211) Although the tourism industry strongly associated Voodoo with the city, only a small number of people are serious adherents.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Dog_Park_at_NOLA_City_Park_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Dog_Park_at_NOLA_City_Park_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dog_Park_at_NOLA_City_Park_-_panoramio.jpg)

Popp Fountain in City Park, a meeting place for The Religious Order of Witchcraft

New Orleans was also home to the occultist [Mary Oneida Toups](/wiki/Mary_Oneida_Toups "Mary Oneida Toups"), who was nicknamed the "Witch Queen of New Orleans". Toups' coven, The Religious Order of Witchcraft, was the first coven to be officially recognized as a religious institution by the state of Louisiana.[[207]](#cite_note-212) They would meet at Popp Fountain in [City Park](/wiki/City_Park_(New_Orleans) "City Park (New Orleans)").[[208]](#cite_note-213)

Jewish settlers, primarily [Sephardim](/wiki/Sephardim "Sephardim"), settled in New Orleans from the early nineteenth century. Some migrated from the communities established in the colonial years in [Charleston, South Carolina](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina"), and [Savannah, Georgia](/wiki/Savannah,_Georgia "Savannah, Georgia"). The merchant [Abraham Cohen Labatt](/wiki/Abraham_Cohen_Labatt "Abraham Cohen Labatt") helped found the first Jewish congregation in New Orleans in the 1830s, which became known as the [Portuguese Jewish](/wiki/Spanish_and_Portuguese_Jews "Spanish and Portuguese Jews") Nefutzot Yehudah congregation (he and some other members were [Sephardic Jews](/wiki/Sephardic_Jews "Sephardic Jews"), whose ancestors had lived in Portugal and Spain). [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews") from eastern Europe immigrated in the late 19th and 20th centuries.

By the beginning of the 21st century, 10,000 [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew") lived in New Orleans. This number dropped to 7,000 after Hurricane Katrina, but rose again after efforts to incentivize the community's growth resulted in the arrival of about an additional 2,000 Jews.[[209]](#cite_note-214) New Orleans synagogues lost members, but most re-opened in their original locations. The exception was [Congregation Beth Israel](/wiki/Congregation_Beth_Israel_(New_Orleans) "Congregation Beth Israel (New Orleans)"), the oldest and most prominent [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") synagogue in the New Orleans region. Beth Israel's building in Lakeview was destroyed by flooding. After seven years of holding services in temporary quarters, the congregation consecrated a new synagogue on land purchased from the [Reform](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") Congregation Gates of Prayer in [Metairie](/wiki/Metairie,_Louisiana "Metairie, Louisiana").[[210]](#cite_note-Beth_Israel_7_years-215)

A visible religious minority,[[211]](#cite_note-216)[[212]](#cite_note-217) [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") constituted 0.6% of the religious population as of 2019 according to [Sperling's BestPlaces](/wiki/Sperling%27s_BestPlaces "Sperling's BestPlaces").[[213]](#cite_note-:4-218) The Association of Religion Data Archives in 2020 estimated that there were 6,150 Muslims in the city proper. The Islamic demographic in New Orleans and its metropolitan area have been mainly made up of Middle Eastern immigrants and [African Americans](/wiki/African-American_Muslims "African-American Muslims").

## Economy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Tanker_IVER_SPRING_on_Mississippi_River_in_New_Orleans.jpeg/250px-Tanker_IVER_SPRING_on_Mississippi_River_in_New_Orleans.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Tanker_IVER_SPRING_on_Mississippi_River_in_New_Orleans.jpeg)

A [tanker](/wiki/Tank_ship "Tank ship") on the Mississippi River in New Orleans

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Intracoastal_Waterway_Louisiana.jpg/250px-Intracoastal_Waterway_Louisiana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Intracoastal_Waterway_Louisiana.jpg)

[Intracoastal Waterway](/wiki/Intracoastal_Waterway "Intracoastal Waterway") near New Orleans

New Orleans operates [one of the world's largest and busiest ports](/wiki/Port_of_New_Orleans "Port of New Orleans") and metropolitan New Orleans is a center of maritime industry.[[214]](#cite_note-219) The region accounts for a significant portion of the nation's [oil refining](/wiki/Oil_refinery "Oil refinery") and [petrochemical production](/wiki/Petrochemical "Petrochemical"), and serves as a [white-collar](/wiki/White-collar_worker "White-collar worker") corporate base for onshore and offshore [petroleum](/wiki/Extraction_of_petroleum "Extraction of petroleum") and [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") production. Since the beginning of the 21st century, New Orleans has also grown into a technology hub.[[215]](#cite_note-220)[[216]](#cite_note-221)

New Orleans is also a center for [higher learning](/wiki/Higher_learning "Higher learning"), with over 50,000 students enrolled in the region's eleven two- and four-year degree-granting institutions. [Tulane University](/wiki/Tulane_University "Tulane University"), a top-50 research university, is located in Uptown. Metropolitan New Orleans is a major regional hub for the [health care industry](/wiki/Health_care_industry "Health care industry") and boasts a small, globally competitive manufacturing sector. The center city possesses a rapidly growing, entrepreneurial [creative industries](/wiki/Creative_industries "Creative industries") sector and is renowned for its [cultural tourism](/wiki/Cultural_tourism "Cultural tourism"). Greater New Orleans, Inc. (GNO, Inc.)[[217]](#cite_note-222) acts as the first point-of-contact for regional economic development, coordinating between Louisiana's Department of Economic Development and the various business development agencies.

### Port

New Orleans began as a strategically located trading *[entrepôt](/wiki/Entrep%C3%B4t "Entrepôt")* and it remains, above all, a crucial transportation hub and distribution center for waterborne commerce. The [Port of New Orleans](/wiki/Port_of_New_Orleans "Port of New Orleans") is the fifth-largest in the United States based on cargo volume, and second-largest in the state after the [Port of South Louisiana](/wiki/Port_of_South_Louisiana "Port of South Louisiana"). It is the twelfth-largest in the U.S. based on cargo value. The Port of South Louisiana, also located in the New Orleans area, is the world's busiest in terms of bulk tonnage. When combined with Port of New Orleans, it forms the 4th-largest port system in volume. Many shipbuilding, shipping, logistics, freight forwarding and commodity brokerage firms either are based in metropolitan New Orleans or maintain a local presence. Examples include [Intermarine](/wiki/Intermarine "Intermarine"),[[218]](#cite_note-223) Bisso Towboat,[[219]](#cite_note-224) [Northrop Grumman Ship Systems](/wiki/Northrop_Grumman_Ship_Systems "Northrop Grumman Ship Systems"),[[220]](#cite_note-225) Trinity Yachts, [Expeditors International](/wiki/Expeditors_International "Expeditors International"),[[221]](#cite_note-226) Bollinger Shipyards, IMTT, International Coffee Corp, Boasso America, Transoceanic Shipping, Transportation Consultants Inc., Dupuy Storage & Forwarding and Silocaf.[[222]](#cite_note-227) The largest coffee-roasting plant in the world, operated by [Folgers](/wiki/Folgers "Folgers"), is located in [New Orleans East](/wiki/Eastern_New_Orleans "Eastern New Orleans").[[223]](#cite_note-228)[[224]](#cite_note-229)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Boats_on_the_Mississippi_%282965404740%29.jpg/250px-Boats_on_the_Mississippi_%282965404740%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boats_on_the_Mississippi_(2965404740).jpg)

The steamboat [*Natchez*](/wiki/Natchez_(boat) "Natchez (boat)") operates out of New Orleans.

New Orleans is located near to the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") and its many oil rigs. Louisiana ranks fifth among states in oil production and eighth in [reserves](/wiki/Oil_reserves "Oil reserves"). It has two of the four [Strategic Petroleum Reserve](/wiki/Strategic_Petroleum_Reserve_(United_States) "Strategic Petroleum Reserve (United States)") (SPR) storage facilities: West Hackberry in [Cameron Parish](/wiki/Cameron_Parish,_Louisiana "Cameron Parish, Louisiana") and Bayou Choctaw in [Iberville Parish](/wiki/Iberville_Parish,_Louisiana "Iberville Parish, Louisiana"). The area hosts 17 petroleum refineries, with a combined crude oil distillation capacity of nearly 2.8 million barrels per day (450,000 m3/d), the second highest after Texas. Louisiana's numerous ports include the [Louisiana Offshore Oil Port](/wiki/Louisiana_Offshore_Oil_Port "Louisiana Offshore Oil Port") (LOOP), which is capable of receiving the largest oil tankers. Given the quantity of oil imports, Louisiana is home to many major pipelines: [Crude Oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") ([Exxon](/wiki/Exxon "Exxon"), [Chevron](/wiki/Chevron_Corporation "Chevron Corporation"), [BP](/wiki/BP "BP"), [Texaco](/wiki/Texaco "Texaco"), [Shell](/wiki/Shell_Oil_Company "Shell Oil Company"), Scurloch-Permian, Mid-Valley, Calumet, [Conoco](/wiki/Conoco_Inc. "Conoco Inc."), [Koch Inc.](/w/index.php?title=Koch_Inc.&action=edit&redlink=1 "Koch Inc. (page does not exist)"), [Unocal](/wiki/Unocal_Corporation "Unocal Corporation"), [U.S. Dept. of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy"), Locap); Product ([TEPPCO Partners](/wiki/TEPPCO_Partners "TEPPCO Partners"), Colonial, Plantation, Explorer, Texaco, Collins); and [Liquefied Petroleum Gas](/wiki/Liquefied_petroleum_gas "Liquefied petroleum gas") (Dixie, TEPPCO, Black Lake, Koch, Chevron, [Dynegy](/wiki/Dynegy "Dynegy"), [Kinder Morgan Energy Partners](/wiki/Kinder_Morgan_Energy_Partners "Kinder Morgan Energy Partners"), [Dow Chemical Company](/wiki/Dow_Chemical_Company "Dow Chemical Company"), Bridgeline, FMP, Tejas, Texaco, UTP).[[225]](#cite_note-230) Several energy companies have regional headquarters in the area, including [Shell plc](/wiki/Shell_plc "Shell plc"), [Eni](/wiki/Eni "Eni") and Chevron. Other energy producers and [oilfield services companies](/wiki/Oilfield_services "Oilfield services") are headquartered in the city or region, and the sector supports a large professional services base of specialized engineering and design firms, as well as a term office for the federal government's [Minerals Management Service](/wiki/Minerals_Management_Service "Minerals Management Service").

### Business

The city is the home to a single [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") company: [Entergy](/wiki/Entergy "Entergy"), a power generation utility and [nuclear power plant](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant") operations specialist.[[226]](#cite_note-231) After Katrina, the city lost its other Fortune 500 company, [Freeport-McMoRan](/wiki/Freeport-McMoRan "Freeport-McMoRan"), when it merged its copper and gold exploration unit with an Arizona company and relocated that division to [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"). Its McMoRan Exploration affiliate remains headquartered in New Orleans.[[227]](#cite_note-232)

Companies with significant operations or headquarters in New Orleans include: Pan American Life Insurance, Pool Corp, [Rolls-Royce](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_plc "Rolls-Royce plc"), Newpark Resources, [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T_Inc. "AT&T Inc."), TurboSquid, iSeatz, [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), Navtech, [Superior Energy Services](/wiki/Superior_Energy_Services "Superior Energy Services"), [Textron Marine & Land Systems](/wiki/Textron_Marine_%26_Land_Systems "Textron Marine & Land Systems"), [McDermott International](/wiki/McDermott_International "McDermott International"), Pellerin Milnor, [Lockheed Martin](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin "Lockheed Martin"), Imperial Trading, Laitram, [Harrah's Entertainment](/wiki/Harrah%27s_Entertainment "Harrah's Entertainment"), Stewart Enterprises, [Edison Chouest Offshore](/wiki/Edison_Chouest_Offshore "Edison Chouest Offshore"), [Zatarain's](/wiki/Zatarain%27s "Zatarain's"), Waldemar S. Nelson & Co., [Whitney National Bank](/wiki/Whitney_National_Bank "Whitney National Bank"), [Capital One](/wiki/Capital_One "Capital One"), [Tidewater Marine](/wiki/Tidewater_(marine_services) "Tidewater (marine services)"), [Popeyes Chicken & Biscuits](/wiki/Popeyes_Chicken_%26_Biscuits "Popeyes Chicken & Biscuits"), [Parsons Brinckerhoff](/wiki/Parsons_Brinckerhoff "Parsons Brinckerhoff"), [MWH Global](/wiki/MWH_Global "MWH Global"), [CH2M Hill](/wiki/CH2M_Hill "CH2M Hill"), Energy Partners Ltd, The Receivables Exchange, [GE Capital](/wiki/GE_Capital "GE Capital"), and [Smoothie King](/wiki/Smoothie_King "Smoothie King").

Entrepreneurship is encouraged, with a number of initiatives designed to support small business and start-ups, including [Entrepreneurs' Row](/wiki/Entrepreneurs%27_Row "Entrepreneurs' Row") and the New Orleans Startup Fund.[[228]](#cite_note-233)

### Tourist and convention business

Tourism is a staple of the city's economy. Perhaps more visible than any other sector, New Orleans's tourist and convention industry is a $5.5 billion industry that accounts for 40 percent of city tax revenues. In 2004, the hospitality industry employed 85,000 people, making it the city's top economic sector as measured by employment.[[229]](#cite_note-0607marketing-234) New Orleans also hosts the World Cultural Economic Forum (WCEF). The forum, held annually at the [New Orleans Morial Convention Center](/wiki/New_Orleans_Morial_Convention_Center "New Orleans Morial Convention Center"), is directed toward promoting cultural and economic development opportunities through the strategic convening of cultural ambassadors and leaders from around the world. The first WCEF took place in October 2008.[[230]](#cite_note-235)

### Federal and military agencies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Aerial_View_of_NASA%27s_Rocket_Factory.png/250px-Aerial_View_of_NASA%27s_Rocket_Factory.png)](/wiki/File:Aerial_View_of_NASA%27s_Rocket_Factory.png)

Aerial view of NASA's Michoud Assembly Facility

Federal agencies and the Armed forces operate significant facilities there. The [U.S. Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Fifth_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit") operates at the US. Courthouse downtown. [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA")'s [Michoud Assembly Facility](/wiki/Michoud_Assembly_Facility "Michoud Assembly Facility") is located in [New Orleans East](/wiki/Eastern_New_Orleans "Eastern New Orleans") and has multiple tenants including [Lockheed Martin](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin "Lockheed Martin") and [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing"). It is a huge manufacturing complex that produced the external fuel tanks for the [Space Shuttles](/wiki/Space_Shuttle "Space Shuttle"), the [Saturn V](/wiki/Saturn_V "Saturn V") first stage, the [Integrated Truss Structure](/wiki/Integrated_Truss_Structure "Integrated Truss Structure") of the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station"), and is now used for the construction of NASA's [Space Launch System](/wiki/Space_Launch_System "Space Launch System"). The rocket factory lies within the enormous New Orleans Regional Business Park, also home to the [National Finance Center](/wiki/National_Finance_Center "National Finance Center"), operated by the [United States Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture") (USDA), and the Crescent Crown distribution center. Other large governmental installations include the U.S. Navy's [Space and Naval Warfare (SPAWAR) Systems Command](/wiki/Space_and_Naval_Warfare_Systems_Command "Space and Naval Warfare Systems Command"), located within the University of New Orleans Research and Technology Park in [Gentilly](/wiki/Gentilly,_New_Orleans "Gentilly, New Orleans"), [Naval Air Station Joint Reserve Base New Orleans](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Joint_Reserve_Base_New_Orleans "Naval Air Station Joint Reserve Base New Orleans"); and the headquarters for the Marine Force Reserves in Federal City in [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers,_New_Orleans "Algiers, New Orleans").

## Culture and contemporary life

Main article: [Culture of New Orleans](/wiki/Culture_of_New_Orleans "Culture of New Orleans")

### Tourism

See also: [Culture of New Orleans § Museums and other attractions](/wiki/Culture_of_New_Orleans#Museums_and_other_attractions "Culture of New Orleans")

New Orleans has many visitor attractions, from the world-renowned French Quarter to [St. Charles Avenue](/wiki/St._Charles_Avenue "St. Charles Avenue"), (home of Tulane and Loyola universities, the historic [Pontchartrain Hotel](/wiki/Pontchartrain_Hotel "Pontchartrain Hotel") and many 19th-century mansions) to [Magazine Street](/wiki/Magazine_Street "Magazine Street") with its boutique stores and antique shops.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/French_Quarter03_New_Orleans.JPG/250px-French_Quarter03_New_Orleans.JPG)](/wiki/File:French_Quarter03_New_Orleans.JPG)

The [French Quarter](/wiki/French_Quarter "French Quarter") in 2009

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/New-Orleans-Street-Artist-1988-205.jpg/330px-New-Orleans-Street-Artist-1988-205.jpg)](/wiki/File:New-Orleans-Street-Artist-1988-205.jpg)

A [street artist](/wiki/Street_artist "Street artist") in the French Quarter, 1988

According to current travel guides, New Orleans is one of the top ten most-visited cities in the United States; 10.1 million visitors came to New Orleans in 2004.[[229]](#cite_note-0607marketing-234)[[231]](#cite_note-236) Prior to Katrina, 265 hotels with 38,338 rooms operated in the Greater New Orleans Area. In May 2007, that had declined to some 140 hotels and motels with over 31,000 rooms.[[232]](#cite_note-New_Orleans'_Recovery_as_of_May_2007-237)

A 2009 *[Travel + Leisure](/wiki/Travel_%2B_Leisure "Travel + Leisure")* poll of "America's Favorite Cities" ranked New Orleans first in ten categories, the most first-place rankings of the 30 cities included. According to the poll, New Orleans was the best U.S. city as a spring break destination and for "wild weekends", stylish boutique hotels, cocktail hours, singles/bar scenes, live music/concerts and bands, antique and vintage shops, cafés/coffee bars, neighborhood restaurants, and [people watching](/wiki/People_watching "People watching"). The city ranked second for: friendliness (behind [Charleston, South Carolina](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina")), [gay](/wiki/Gay "Gay")-friendliness (behind San Francisco), [bed and breakfast](/wiki/Bed_and_breakfast "Bed and breakfast") hotels/inns, and ethnic food. However, the city placed near the bottom in cleanliness, safety and as a family destination.[[233]](#cite_note-238)[[234]](#cite_note-239)

The French Quarter (known locally as "the Quarter" or *Vieux Carré*), which was the colonial-era city and is bounded by the Mississippi River, Rampart Street, [Canal Street](/wiki/Canal_Street,_New_Orleans "Canal Street, New Orleans"), and [Esplanade Avenue](/wiki/Esplanade_Avenue,_New_Orleans "Esplanade Avenue, New Orleans"), contains popular hotels, bars and nightclubs. Notable tourist attractions in the Quarter include Bourbon Street, Jackson Square, St. Louis Cathedral, the [French Market](/wiki/French_Market "French Market") (including [Café du Monde](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9_du_Monde "Café du Monde"), famous for [café au lait](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9_au_lait "Café au lait") and [beignets](/wiki/Beignet "Beignet")) and [Preservation Hall](/wiki/Preservation_Hall "Preservation Hall"). Also in the French Quarter is the old [New Orleans Mint](/wiki/New_Orleans_Mint "New Orleans Mint"), a former branch of the [United States Mint](/wiki/United_States_Mint "United States Mint") which now operates as a museum, and [The Historic New Orleans Collection](/wiki/The_Historic_New_Orleans_Collection "The Historic New Orleans Collection"), a museum and research center housing art and artifacts relating to the [history](/wiki/History_of_New_Orleans "History of New Orleans") and the [Gulf South](/wiki/Gulf_South "Gulf South").

Close to the Quarter is the [Tremé](/wiki/Trem%C3%A9 "Tremé") community, which contains the [New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park](/wiki/New_Orleans_Jazz_National_Historical_Park "New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park") and the [New Orleans African American Museum](/wiki/New_Orleans_African_American_Museum "New Orleans African American Museum")—a site which is listed on the [Louisiana African American Heritage Trail](/wiki/Louisiana_African_American_Heritage_Trail "Louisiana African American Heritage Trail").

The *[Natchez](/wiki/Natchez_(boat) "Natchez (boat)")* is an authentic [steamboat](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") with a [calliope](/wiki/Calliope_(music) "Calliope (music)") that cruises the length of the city twice daily. Unlike most other places in the United States, New Orleans has become widely known for its [elegant decay](/wiki/Elegant_decay "Elegant decay"). The city's [historic cemeteries](/wiki/Historic_Cemeteries_of_New_Orleans "Historic Cemeteries of New Orleans") and their distinct above-ground [tombs](/wiki/Tomb "Tomb") are attractions in themselves, the oldest and most famous of which, [Saint Louis Cemetery](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Cemetery "Saint Louis Cemetery"), greatly resembles [Père Lachaise Cemetery](/wiki/P%C3%A8re_Lachaise_Cemetery "Père Lachaise Cemetery") in Paris.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/NOMAReopeningDayDelgadoFascade.jpg/330px-NOMAReopeningDayDelgadoFascade.jpg)](/wiki/File:NOMAReopeningDayDelgadoFascade.jpg)

The [New Orleans Museum of Art](/wiki/New_Orleans_Museum_of_Art "New Orleans Museum of Art") (NOMA) located in [City Park](/wiki/City_Park,_New_Orleans "City Park, New Orleans")

[The National WWII Museum](/wiki/The_National_WWII_Museum "The National WWII Museum") offers a multi-building odyssey through the history of the Pacific and European theaters. Nearby, [Confederate Memorial Hall Museum](/wiki/Confederate_Memorial_Hall_Museum "Confederate Memorial Hall Museum"), the oldest continually operating museum in Louisiana (although under renovation since Hurricane Katrina), contains the second-largest collection of [Confederate](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") memorabilia. Art museums include the [Contemporary Arts Center](/wiki/Contemporary_Arts_Center_(New_Orleans) "Contemporary Arts Center (New Orleans)"), the [New Orleans Museum of Art](/wiki/New_Orleans_Museum_of_Art "New Orleans Museum of Art") (NOMA) in [City Park](/wiki/City_Park_(New_Orleans) "City Park (New Orleans)"), and the [Ogden Museum of Southern Art](/wiki/Ogden_Museum_of_Southern_Art "Ogden Museum of Southern Art").

New Orleans is home to the [Audubon Nature Institute](/wiki/Audubon_Nature_Institute "Audubon Nature Institute") (which consists of [Audubon Park](/wiki/Audubon_Park_(New_Orleans) "Audubon Park (New Orleans)"), the [Audubon Zoo](/wiki/Audubon_Zoo "Audubon Zoo"), the [Aquarium of the Americas](/wiki/Aquarium_of_the_Americas "Aquarium of the Americas") and the [Audubon Insectarium](/wiki/Audubon_Insectarium "Audubon Insectarium")), and home to gardens which include [Longue Vue House and Gardens](/wiki/Longue_Vue_House_and_Gardens "Longue Vue House and Gardens") and the [New Orleans Botanical Garden](/wiki/New_Orleans_Botanical_Garden "New Orleans Botanical Garden"). City Park, one of the country's most expansive and visited [urban parks](/wiki/Urban_park "Urban park"), has one of the largest stands of [oak trees](/wiki/Southern_live_oak "Southern live oak") in the world.

Other points of interest can be found in the surrounding areas. Many wetlands are found nearby, including [Honey Island Swamp](/wiki/Honey_Island_Swamp "Honey Island Swamp") and [Barataria Preserve](/wiki/Jean_Lafitte_National_Historical_Park_and_Preserve#Barataria_Preserve "Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve"). [Chalmette Battlefield and National Cemetery](/wiki/Jean_Lafitte_National_Historical_Park_and_Preserve#Chalmette_unit "Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve"), located just south of the city, is the site of the 1815 [Battle of New Orleans](/wiki/Battle_of_New_Orleans "Battle of New Orleans").

### Entertainment and performing arts

Main article: [Music of New Orleans](/wiki/Music_of_New_Orleans "Music of New Orleans")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/MardiGrasCanalStreet1890sChartresGazebo.jpg/250px-MardiGrasCanalStreet1890sChartresGazebo.jpg)](/wiki/File:MardiGrasCanalStreet1890sChartresGazebo.jpg)

[New Orleans Mardi Gras](/wiki/New_Orleans_Mardi_Gras "New Orleans Mardi Gras") in the early 1890s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/ToHorses.jpg/250px-ToHorses.jpg)](/wiki/File:ToHorses.jpg)

Mounted [krewe](/wiki/Krewe "Krewe") officers in the Thoth Parade during [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras")

The New Orleans area is home to numerous annual celebrations. The most well known is [Carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival"), or [Mardi Gras](/wiki/New_Orleans_Mardi_Gras "New Orleans Mardi Gras"). Carnival officially begins on the [Feast of the Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)"), also known in some Christian traditions as the "[Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night_(holiday) "Twelfth Night (holiday)")" of Christmas. *Mardi Gras* (French for "Fat Tuesday"), the final and grandest day of traditional Catholic festivities, is the last Tuesday before the [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") liturgical season of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), which commences on [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday").

The largest of the city's many music festivals is the [New Orleans Jazz & Heritage Festival](/wiki/New_Orleans_Jazz_%26_Heritage_Festival "New Orleans Jazz & Heritage Festival"). Commonly referred to simply as "Jazz Fest", it is one of the nation's largest music festivals. The festival features a variety of music, including both native Louisiana and international artists. Along with Jazz Fest, New Orleans's [Voodoo Experience](/wiki/Voodoo_Experience "Voodoo Experience") ("Voodoo Fest") and the [Essence Music Festival](/wiki/Essence_Music_Festival "Essence Music Festival") also feature local and international artists.

Other major festivals include [Southern Decadence](/wiki/Southern_Decadence "Southern Decadence"), the French Quarter Festival, and the [Tennessee Williams/New Orleans Literary Festival](/wiki/Tennessee_Williams/New_Orleans_Literary_Festival "Tennessee Williams/New Orleans Literary Festival"). The American playwright lived and wrote in New Orleans early in his career, and set his play, *[Streetcar Named Desire](/wiki/Streetcar_Named_Desire "Streetcar Named Desire"),* there.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Louis_Prima_crop.jpg/250px-Louis_Prima_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis_Prima_crop.jpg)

[Louis Prima](/wiki/Louis_Prima "Louis Prima"); a famous New Orleans [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [swing music](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music"), and [jump blues](/wiki/Jump_blues "Jump blues"), musician.

In 2002, Louisiana began offering tax incentives for film and television production. This has resulted in a substantial increase in activity and brought the nickname of "Hollywood South" for New Orleans. Films produced in and around the city include *[Ray](/wiki/Ray_(film) "Ray (film)")*, *[Runaway Jury](/wiki/Runaway_Jury "Runaway Jury")*, *[The Pelican Brief](/wiki/The_Pelican_Brief_(film) "The Pelican Brief (film)")*, *[Glory Road](/wiki/Glory_Road_(film) "Glory Road (film)")*, *[All the King's Men](/wiki/All_the_King%27s_Men_(2006_film) "All the King's Men (2006 film)")*, *[Déjà Vu](/wiki/Deja_Vu_(2006_film) "Deja Vu (2006 film)")*, *[Last Holiday](/wiki/Last_Holiday_(2006_film) "Last Holiday (2006 film)")*, *[The Curious Case of Benjamin Button](/wiki/The_Curious_Case_of_Benjamin_Button_(film) "The Curious Case of Benjamin Button (film)")*, *[12 Years a Slave](/wiki/12_Years_a_Slave_(film) "12 Years a Slave (film)")*, and *[Project Power](/wiki/Project_Power "Project Power")*. In 2006, work began on the Louisiana Film & Television studio complex, based in the [Tremé](/wiki/Trem%C3%A9 "Tremé") neighborhood.[[235]](#cite_note-240) Louisiana began to offer similar tax incentives for music and theater productions in 2007, and some commentators began to refer to New Orleans as "Broadway South".[[236]](#cite_note-241)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Louis_Armstrong_in_Color_%28restored%29.jpg/250px-Louis_Armstrong_in_Color_%28restored%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis_Armstrong_in_Color_(restored).jpg)

[Louis Armstrong](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong "Louis Armstrong"), famous New Orleans [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") musician

The first theatre in New Orleans was the French-language [Theatre de la Rue Saint Pierre](/wiki/Theatre_de_la_Rue_Saint_Pierre "Theatre de la Rue Saint Pierre"), which opened in 1792. The first opera in New Orleans was performed there in 1796. In the nineteenth century, the city was the home of two of America's most important venues for [French opera](/wiki/French_opera "French opera"), the [Théâtre d'Orléans](/wiki/Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_d%27Orl%C3%A9ans "Théâtre d'Orléans") and later the [French Opera House](/wiki/French_Opera_House "French Opera House"). Today, opera is performed by the [New Orleans Opera](/wiki/New_Orleans_Opera "New Orleans Opera"). The [Marigny Opera House](/wiki/Marigny_Opera_House "Marigny Opera House") is home to the Marigny Opera Ballet and also hosts opera, jazz, and classical music performances.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Frank_Ocean.jpg/250px-Frank_Ocean.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Ocean.jpg)

[Frank Ocean](/wiki/Frank_Ocean "Frank Ocean") is a musician from New Orleans.

New Orleans has long been a significant center for music, showcasing its intertwined European, African and Latino American cultures. The city's unique musical heritage was born in its colonial and early American days from a unique blending of European musical instruments with African rhythms. As the only North American city to have allowed [slaves](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States") to gather in public and play their native music (largely in [Congo Square](/wiki/Congo_Square "Congo Square"), now located within [Louis Armstrong Park](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong_Park_(New_Orleans) "Louis Armstrong Park (New Orleans)")), New Orleans gave birth in the early 20th century to an epochal indigenous music: [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"). Soon, African American [brass bands](/wiki/Brass_band "Brass band") formed, beginning a century-long tradition. The Louis Armstrong Park area, near the French Quarter in Tremé, contains the [New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park](/wiki/New_Orleans_Jazz_National_Historical_Park "New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park"). The city's music was later also significantly influenced by [Acadiana](/wiki/Acadiana "Acadiana"), home of [Cajun](/wiki/Cajun_music "Cajun music") and [Zydeco](/wiki/Zydeco "Zydeco") music, and by [Delta blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues").

New Orleans's unique musical culture is on display in its traditional funerals. A spin on military funerals, New Orleans's traditional funerals feature sad music (mostly [dirges](/wiki/Dirge "Dirge") and [hymns](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn")) in processions on the way to the cemetery and happier music (hot jazz) on the way back. Until the 1990s, most locals preferred to call these "funerals with music". Visitors to the city have long dubbed them "[jazz funerals](/wiki/Jazz_funeral "Jazz funeral")".

Much later in its musical development, New Orleans was home to a distinctive brand of [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") that contributed greatly to the growth of [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"). An example of the New Orleans's sound in the 1960s is the No. 1 U.S. hit "[Chapel of Love](/wiki/Chapel_of_Love "Chapel of Love")" by [the Dixie Cups](/wiki/The_Dixie_Cups "The Dixie Cups"), a song which knocked [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") out of the top spot on the [Billboard Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100"). New Orleans became a hotbed for [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") music in the 1960s and 1970s, and by the late 1980s, it had developed its own localized variant of [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music"), called [bounce music](/wiki/Bounce_music "Bounce music"). While not commercially successful outside of the [Deep South](/wiki/Deep_South "Deep South"), bounce music was immensely popular in poorer neighborhoods throughout the 1990s.

A cousin of bounce, [New Orleans hip hop](/wiki/Southern_hip_hop "Southern hip hop") achieved commercial success locally and internationally, producing [Lil Wayne](/wiki/Lil_Wayne "Lil Wayne"), [Master P](/wiki/Master_P "Master P"), [Birdman](/wiki/Birdman_(rapper) "Birdman (rapper)"), [Juvenile](/wiki/Juvenile_(rapper) "Juvenile (rapper)"), [Suicideboys](/wiki/Suicideboys "Suicideboys"), [Cash Money Records](/wiki/Cash_Money_Records "Cash Money Records") and [No Limit Records](/wiki/No_Limit_Records "No Limit Records"). Additionally, the popularity of [cowpunk](/wiki/Cowpunk "Cowpunk"), a fast form of [southern rock](/wiki/Southern_rock "Southern rock"), originated with the help of several local bands, such as [The Radiators](/wiki/The_Radiators_(US) "The Radiators (US)"), [Better Than Ezra](/wiki/Better_Than_Ezra "Better Than Ezra"), [Cowboy Mouth](/wiki/Cowboy_Mouth "Cowboy Mouth") and [Dash Rip Rock](/wiki/Dash_Rip_Rock "Dash Rip Rock"). Throughout the 1990s, many [sludge metal](/wiki/Sludge_metal "Sludge metal") bands started. New Orleans's [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") bands such as [Eyehategod](/wiki/Eyehategod "Eyehategod"),[[237]](#cite_note-AMG-Eyehategod-242) [Soilent Green](/wiki/Soilent_Green "Soilent Green"),[[238]](#cite_note-AMG-SoilentGreen-243) [Crowbar](/wiki/Crowbar_(US_band) "Crowbar (US band)"),[[239]](#cite_note-AMG-Crowbar-244) and [Down](/wiki/Down_(band) "Down (band)") incorporated styles such as [hardcore punk](/wiki/Hardcore_punk "Hardcore punk"),[[240]](#cite_note-AMG-Down-245) [doom metal](/wiki/Doom_metal "Doom metal"), and southern rock to create an original and heady brew of swampy and aggravated metal that has largely avoided standardization.[[237]](#cite_note-AMG-Eyehategod-242)[[238]](#cite_note-AMG-SoilentGreen-243)[[239]](#cite_note-AMG-Crowbar-244)[[240]](#cite_note-AMG-Down-245)

New Orleans is the southern terminus of the famed [Highway 61](/wiki/U.S._Route_61 "U.S. Route 61"), made musically famous by musician [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") in his song, "[Highway 61 Revisited](/wiki/Highway_61_Revisited "Highway 61 Revisited")".

### Cuisine

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Steamship_Bienville_on-board_restaurant_menu_%28April_7%2C_1861%29.jpg/250px-Steamship_Bienville_on-board_restaurant_menu_%28April_7%2C_1861%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Steamship_Bienville_on-board_restaurant_menu_(April_7,_1861).jpg)

Steamship *Bienville* on-board restaurant menu (April 7, 1861)

Main articles: [Cuisine of New Orleans](/wiki/Cuisine_of_New_Orleans "Cuisine of New Orleans"), [Louisiana Creole cuisine](/wiki/Louisiana_Creole_cuisine "Louisiana Creole cuisine"), and [Cajun cuisine](/wiki/Cajun_cuisine "Cajun cuisine")

New Orleans is world-famous for its cuisine. The indigenous cuisine is distinctive and influential. New Orleans food combined local Creole, haute Creole and New Orleans French cuisines. Local ingredients, French, Spanish, Italian, African, Native American, Cajun, Chinese, and a hint of Cuban traditions combine to produce a truly unique and easily recognizable New Orleans flavor.

New Orleans is known for specialties including [beignets](/wiki/Beignet "Beignet") (locally pronounced like "ben-yays"), square-shaped fried dough that could be called "French doughnuts" (served with *[café au lait](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9_au_lait "Café au lait")* made with a blend of coffee and chicory rather than only coffee); and [po' boy](/wiki/Po%27_boy "Po' boy")[[241]](#cite_note-246) and Italian [muffuletta](/wiki/Muffuletta "Muffuletta") sandwiches; Gulf oysters on the half-shell, fried oysters, boiled [crawfish](/wiki/Crayfish "Crayfish") and other [seafood](/wiki/Seafood "Seafood"); [étouffée](/wiki/%C3%89touff%C3%A9e "Étouffée"), [jambalaya](/wiki/Jambalaya "Jambalaya"), [gumbo](/wiki/Gumbo "Gumbo") and other Creole dishes; and the Monday favorite of [red beans and rice](/wiki/Red_beans_and_rice "Red beans and rice") ([Louis Armstrong](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong "Louis Armstrong") often signed his letters, "Red beans and ricely yours"). Another New Orleans specialty is the [praline](/wiki/Pralin "Pralin") locally [/ˈprɑːliːn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), a candy made with brown sugar, granulated sugar, cream, butter, and pecans. The city offers notable street food[[242]](#cite_note-247) including the Asian inspired beef [Yaka mein](/wiki/Yaka_mein "Yaka mein").

### Dialect

Main article: [New Orleans English](/wiki/New_Orleans_English "New Orleans English")

See also: [Culture of New Orleans § Language](/wiki/Culture_of_New_Orleans#Language "Culture of New Orleans")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Cafe_du_Monde_New_Orleans.jpg/250px-Cafe_du_Monde_New_Orleans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cafe_du_Monde_New_Orleans.jpg)

[Café du Monde](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9_du_Monde "Café du Monde"), a landmark New Orleans beignet cafe established in 1862

New Orleans developed a distinctive local dialect that is neither [Cajun English](/wiki/Cajun_English "Cajun English") nor the stereotypical [Southern accent](/wiki/Southern_American_English "Southern American English") that is often misportrayed by film and television actors. Like earlier Southern Englishes, it features frequent [deletion of the pre-consonantal "r"](/wiki/Rhotic_and_non-rhotic_accents "Rhotic and non-rhotic accents"), though the local white dialect also came to be quite similar to [New York accents](/wiki/New_York_accent "New York accent").[[243]](#cite_note-248) No consensus describes how this happened, but it likely resulted from New Orleans's geographic isolation by water and the fact that the city was a major immigration port throughout the 19th century and early 20th century. Specifically, many members of European immigrant families originally raised in the cities of the Northeast, namely New York, moved to New Orleans during this time frame, bringing their Northeastern accents along with their [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American") (especially [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_American "Sicilian American")), [German](/wiki/German_American "German American"), and [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish_American "Jewish American") culture.[[244]](#cite_note-249)

One of the strongest varieties of the New Orleans accent is sometimes identified as the [Yat dialect](/wiki/Yat_dialect "Yat dialect"), from the greeting "Where y'at?" This distinctive accent is dying out in the city, but remains strong in the surrounding parishes.

Less visibly, various ethnic groups throughout the area have retained distinct language traditions. The French-speaking community has had a cultural center, the [Alliance Française of New Orleans](/wiki/Alliance_Fran%C3%A7aise_of_New_Orleans "Alliance Française of New Orleans"), since 1984.[[245]](#cite_note-250) The association is a chapter of the international [Alliance Française](/wiki/Alliance_Fran%C3%A7aise "Alliance Française") organization and promotes French language and culture in New Orleans and the surrounding region. Since Louisiana became the first U.S. state to join the *[Organisation Internationale de la Francophonie](/wiki/Organisation_Internationale_de_la_Francophonie "Organisation Internationale de la Francophonie")* in 2018, New Orleans has reemerged as an important center for the state's francophone and creolophone cultures and languages, as seen in new organizations such as the Nous Foundation.[[246]](#cite_note-251) Although rare, [Louisiana French](/wiki/Louisiana_French "Louisiana French") and [Louisiana Creole](/wiki/Louisiana_Creole "Louisiana Creole") are still spoken in the city. There is also Louisiana-Canarian Spanish dialect, the [Isleño Spanish](/wiki/Isle%C3%B1o_Spanish "Isleño Spanish"), spoken by the [Isleño](/wiki/Isle%C3%B1o "Isleño") people and older members of the population.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Fleur_de_lys_%28or%29.svg/120px-Fleur_de_lys_%28or%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Fleur_de_lys_(or).svg)

The [fleur-de-lis](/wiki/Fleur-de-lis "Fleur-de-lis") is often a symbol of New Orleans and its sports teams.

New Orleans's professional sports teams include the 2009 [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV") champion [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") ([NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")) and the [New Orleans Pelicans](/wiki/New_Orleans_Pelicans "New Orleans Pelicans") ([NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")).[[247]](#cite_note-252)[[248]](#cite_note-253)[[249]](#cite_note-254) It is also home to the [Big Easy Rollergirls](/wiki/Big_Easy_Rollergirls "Big Easy Rollergirls"), an all-female [flat track roller derby](/wiki/Women%27s_Flat_Track_Derby_Association "Women's Flat Track Derby Association") team, and the [New Orleans Blaze](/wiki/New_Orleans_Blaze "New Orleans Blaze"), a [women's football](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Football_Association "National Women's Football Association") team.[[250]](#cite_note-BlazeFootball-255)[[251]](#cite_note-BERG-256) New Orleans is also home to two [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") athletic programs, the [Tulane Green Wave](/wiki/Tulane_Green_Wave "Tulane Green Wave") of the [American Athletic Conference](/wiki/American_Athletic_Conference "American Athletic Conference") and the [UNO Privateers](/wiki/UNO_Privateers "UNO Privateers") of the [Southland Conference](/wiki/Southland_Conference "Southland Conference").

The [Caesars Superdome](/wiki/Caesars_Superdome "Caesars Superdome") is the home of the Saints, the Sugar Bowl, and other prominent events. It has hosted the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") a record eight times ([1978](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XII "Super Bowl XII"), [1981](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XV "Super Bowl XV"), [1986](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XX "Super Bowl XX"), [1990](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXIV "Super Bowl XXIV"), [1997](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXI "Super Bowl XXXI"), [2002](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXVI "Super Bowl XXXVI"), [2013](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVII "Super Bowl XLVII"), and [2025](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX")). The [Smoothie King Center](/wiki/Smoothie_King_Center "Smoothie King Center") is the home of the Pelicans, VooDoo, and many events that are not large enough to need the Superdome. New Orleans is also home to the [Fair Grounds Race Course](/wiki/Fair_Grounds_Race_Course "Fair Grounds Race Course"), the nation's third-oldest thoroughbred track. The city's [Lakefront Arena](/wiki/Lakefront_Arena "Lakefront Arena") has also been home to sporting events.

Each year New Orleans plays host to the [Sugar Bowl](/wiki/Sugar_Bowl "Sugar Bowl"), the [New Orleans Bowl](/wiki/New_Orleans_Bowl "New Orleans Bowl"), the [Bayou Classic](/wiki/Bayou_Classic "Bayou Classic"), and the [Zurich Classic](/wiki/Zurich_Classic_of_New_Orleans "Zurich Classic of New Orleans"), a golf tournament on the [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour"). In addition, it has often hosted major sporting events that have no permanent home, such as the Super Bowl, [ArenaBowl](/wiki/ArenaBowl "ArenaBowl"), [NBA All-Star Game](/wiki/NBA_All-Star_Game "NBA All-Star Game"), [BCS National Championship Game](/wiki/BCS_National_Championship_Game "BCS National Championship Game"), and the [NCAA Final Four](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship"). The [Rock 'n' Roll Mardi Gras Marathon](/wiki/Rock_%27n%27_Roll_Mardi_Gras_Marathon "Rock 'n' Roll Mardi Gras Marathon") and the [Crescent City Classic](/wiki/Crescent_City_Classic "Crescent City Classic") are two annual [road running](/wiki/Road_running "Road running") events.

In 2017, [Major League Rugby](/wiki/Major_League_Rugby "Major League Rugby") had its inaugural season, and [NOLA Gold](/wiki/New_Orleans_Gold "New Orleans Gold") were one of the first teams in the league.[[252]](#cite_note-257) They play at the [Gold Mine on Airline](/wiki/Gold_Mine_on_Airline "Gold Mine on Airline"), a former [minor league baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball") stadium in the suburb of [Metairie](/wiki/Metairie,_Louisiana "Metairie, Louisiana"). In 2022, a consortium started an attempt to bring professional [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") to New Orleans, hoping to place teams in the male [USL Championship](/wiki/USL_Championship "USL Championship") and women's [USL Super League](/wiki/USL_Super_League "USL Super League") by 2025.[[253]](#cite_note-258)
